segments.title: Light fleet carriers lead Britain's Korean War air effort
segments.summary: Meet the light fleet carriers and aviation-support ships that sustained the Royal Navy's Korean War operations.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke identifies Glory, Ocean, Theseus, and Triumph as the light fleet carriers leading Britain's Korean War effort rather than merely supplementing a larger carrier force. Pioneer and Perseus served as maintenance carriers in the pattern of HMS Unicorn, arriving as support ships rather than combat flight decks. Unicorn nevertheless returned fire with her four-inch battery when a shore position engaged her, showing that an aviation-support carrier operating near the coast remained an armed warship. He traces the ships' relatively short front-line careers while noting that Triumph remained useful for decades, eventually becoming a forward aviation repair ship. Those service histories show how quickly wartime-built light carriers moved from front-line operation to reserve, disposal, or specialized support. The passage presents carrier warfare as a system of combat decks, maintenance ships, and forward repair capacity rather than a story of embarked aircraft alone.

evidence.note: The transcript names the combat and maintenance carriers, recounts Unicorn returning four-inch fire, and outlines their service careers.

segments.title: Colossus-class dimensions expose the cost of aircraft growth
segments.summary: Carrier dimensions and machinery lead into a warning that larger postwar aircraft rapidly consume a ship's nominal air-group capacity.
segments.body: The Colossus-class light fleet carriers displaced about 13,200 tons standard and were intended to make 25 knots from 40,000 shaft horsepower on two shafts. Their Unicorn-derived arrangement turned an aviation-support lineage into a fighting carrier, but still required more than a thousand crew and left limited room for future growth. A capacity of up to 52 aircraft was a 1945 measure because later jets and even larger propeller aircraft consumed more deck, hangar, and handling space. Dr. Clarke uses that shrinkage to show why a nominal air-group figure cannot be separated from the aircraft generation it describes. His Queen Elizabeth-class aside makes the design lesson explicit: spare hull and deck capacity help a carrier preserve a useful air group as aviation technology grows.

evidence.note: The transcript gives the carriers' principal characteristics and explains how aircraft growth reduces capacity.

segments.title: Light fleet carriers become other navies' fleet carriers
segments.summary: See how British light carrier designs supplied front-line aviation capability to several postwar navies.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke follows the British light-carrier family into Australian, Canadian, Indian, French, Argentine, and Brazilian service. Ships conceived as light carriers could become the principal fleet carriers of smaller navies, turning British wartime construction into decades of postwar naval aviation capacity. Brazil's Minas Gerais remained in service until 2001, a striking example of the design's longevity outside the Royal Navy. He contrasts that diffusion with the early-1950s force hierarchy: the US Navy was largest, the Royal Navy came next, other Western navies followed, and the Soviet fleet was still rising. The watch point therefore links exported ships to the unequal distribution of carrier aviation after World War II.

evidence.note: The transcript describes export service and compares the scale of postwar naval aviation forces.

segments.title: Seafire FR.47 brings a wartime fighter lineage to Korea
segments.summary: The Seafire FR.47's performance, armament, range, and Korean War service reveal both its potency and its carrier limitations.
segments.body: The FR.47 was closely related to the late-mark Spitfire and served with 800 Naval Air Squadron aboard HMS Triumph during the Malayan Emergency and Korean War. Its 452 mph top speed, rapid climb, and four 20 mm cannon made it a potent interceptor by piston-fighter standards. It could also carry two 500-pound bombs, giving a carrier air group a useful attack option. Yet its internal-fuel combat radius was only about 405 miles, while fitting a large drop tank constrained the bomb load. Dr. Clarke therefore treats the Seafire as capable but operationally compromised, helping explain why the Fleet Air Arm wanted a more durable, longer-legged postwar fighter.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the FR.47's service, performance, weapons, and range.

segments.title: Fairey Firefly combines strike, night, and anti-submarine roles
segments.summary: Firefly performance and payload figures illustrate the value of a versatile two-seat carrier aircraft in Korea-era operations.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke presents the Firefly as a much-improved answer to the operational needs once met by the Fulmar. In fighter configuration it could reach about 386 mph, although its climb was noticeably slower than the Seafire's. Four 20 mm cannon, up to sixteen rockets, or 2,000 pounds of bombs gave it substantial striking power. Radar, radio, and night-flying equipment extended that usefulness into attack, suppression, and anti-submarine work, while its internal-fuel combat range was about 760 miles. The figures explain why a slower aircraft could remain valuable: the Firefly exchanged some interceptor performance for range, mission equipment, and a much broader set of carrier tasks.

evidence.note: The transcript details the Firefly's range, armament, radar, and multiple operational roles.

segments.title: Sea Fury proves a piston fighter still matters in Korea
segments.summary: Ground attack, artillery spotting, patrols, and a MiG-15 victory show the Sea Fury's breadth at the edge of the jet age.
segments.body: The Royal Navy adopted the Sea Fury because its aging Seafires lacked endurance and durability, even as the RAF chose to wait for jets. With a top speed around 460 mph, a combat range near 780 miles, four 20 mm cannon, rockets, and bombs, it offered both fighter performance and useful strike capacity. In Korea, British and Australian Sea Furies flew ground attack, combat air patrol, and artillery-spotting missions. Dr. Clarke recounts the August 1952 engagement in which aircraft from HMS Ocean fought eight MiG-15s while an accompanying Firefly was badly damaged. At least one jet fighter was destroyed and the Sea Furies escaped unharmed, although the transcript notes uncertainty over which pilot's cannon fire earned the victory. The episode shows that a mature piston fighter could still be tactically dangerous during the early jet transition.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Sea Fury adoption, Korean War roles, and the disputed attribution of a MiG-15 shootdown.

segments.title: Skyraider interest and Sea Vampire training expose the straight-deck transition
segments.summary: British interest in the AD-4 Skyraider and Sea Vampire training reveal how the Fleet Air Arm approached the transition from piston aircraft and straight decks to jets.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke first notes that Britain was looking seriously at the AD-4 Skyraider, a reminder that capable piston strike aircraft still attracted attention during the jet transition. He then shows Eric 'Winkle' Brown landing a Sea Vampire on HMS Ocean. The Fleet Air Arm kept its Sea Vampires in a 700-series jet-induction, training, and trials unit rather than sending them into combat, while 800-series squadrons remained the operational formations. Their use nevertheless exposed the difficulty of operating faster-approaching jets from the long straight decks of light fleet carriers. Quoting Caspar John, Dr. Clarke contrasts the new communist jet threat with the piston aircraft carried by Triumph, Theseus, Glory, and Ocean. Korea consequently becomes an argument for both better aircraft and a carrier force redesigned around jet operating demands.

evidence.note: The transcript notes British interest in the AD-4 Skyraider, shows Eric Brown landing a Sea Vampire, distinguishes training from combat squadrons, and links jet approach speeds to straight-deck limitations.

segments.title: Jet speed collapses radar warning time
segments.summary: A simple warning-time comparison explains why fleets had to push radar coverage and combat air patrols farther from the carrier.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses an illustrative warning-time comparison: an aircraft detected 80 miles away at 400 mph gives a fleet roughly twelve minutes to react, while a 1,200 mph attacker reduces that interval to only a few minutes. Twelve minutes may allow fighters to scramble or an existing combat air patrol to turn toward the threat. In the faster case, the attacker may pass a patrol positioned over the carrier or radar picket before it can make an interception. The problem is therefore not only fighter speed but the geometry of the whole defensive system. Radar coverage, pickets, and combat air patrols all have to move outward if the fleet is to regain useful decision and interception time.

evidence.note: The transcript compares warning times and explains the resulting need to push combat air patrols outward.

segments.title: Saunders-Roe SR.A/1 explores a carrier-independent jet fighter
segments.summary: The SR.A/1 flying-boat fighter shows how seriously Britain explored unconventional ways to deploy jets at sea.
segments.body: The Saunders-Roe SR.A/1 offered a jet fighter able to operate from water without depending on a carrier flight deck. Its trials even showed that occasional water ingestion did not automatically flame out the engines, answering an obvious practical concern about the flying-boat arrangement. Technical promise did not produce an operational sponsor, however: the Royal Navy would not pay for it and the Royal Air Force did not need it. That institutional gap matters because a carrier-independent jet fighter might have bypassed some of the straight-deck limitations raised earlier in the video. The design is therefore a revealing technological branch, but not the solution the fleet adopted for its air-defence problem.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the flying-boat jet concept, engine behavior, and lack of service funding.
